title: Cassoulet
subtitle: The great slow-baked bean and meat dish of the French southwest.
excerpt: Haricot beans slow-cooked with duck legs, pork sausages and bacon in a herbed stock, then oven-baked under a breadcrumb crust that is broken and re-formed three times to build up a thick, savoury top. A weekend project for a cold day. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Cassoulet of the Languedoc. The three cities of Carcassonne, Castelnaudary and Toulouse dispute the canonical recipe; this version follows the Castelnaudary tradition of slow-baking with repeated crust-breaking. Julia Child's Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and Elizabeth David's French Provincial Cooking (1960) provide the principal English-language references.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the final breadcrumb crust, after the beans are fully cooked. Defrost overnight; re-add crust and bake fresh.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The bean and meat base improves significantly if made the day before. Bake with the crust on the day of serving.

content.text: Cassoulet is a weekend project, not a weeknight dinner. The beans need soaking. The duck legs need browning. The crust needs breaking and re-forming three or four times during the oven. This is Saturday cooking: slow, aromatic, methodical. The result is something the stove cannot produce — a dish where every component has exchanged flavour with every other over many hours.

attrs.heading: Dried beans
attrs.body: Soak the haricot beans overnight in cold water. Drain and rinse before cooking. Pre-soaked beans give a creamier result than tinned. If using tinned, reduce the initial bean cooking time to 20 minutes.

content.text: Drain the soaked {{haricot-beans}} of beans. Cover with fresh cold water and bring to the boil. Simmer for 45 minutes until almost but not completely tender. Drain and set aside.

Heat {{duck-fat}} of duck fat in a large casserole over medium-high heat. Season {{duck-legs}} duck legs with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper; brown for 4 minutes per side. Remove. Brown {{sausages-pork}} of sausages until coloured; remove. Cook {{back-bacon}} of bacon lardons for 3 minutes; remove.

In the same pot, cook {{onion}} chopped onion and {{carrot}} diced carrot for 8 minutes over medium heat. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic; cook 2 minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{stock-chicken}} of stock, {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves. Add the beans, sausages and bacon. Nestle the duck legs on top.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Scatter {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs over the surface in an even layer. Place the casserole uncovered in the oven. After 30 minutes, break the crust with a spoon and push it into the beans. Repeat this every 30 minutes for a total of three or four times over 2 to 2.5 hours. The final crust should be thick and golden. The beans should be completely tender and the liquid reduced to a thick sauce.

items.cause: Not soaked, or old dried beans, or acid in the sauce preventing softening.
items.fix: Old beans can take much longer. Add the tomatoes only after the beans are mostly tender; acid prevents them softening.

items.symptom: Casserole drying out in the oven
items.cause: Too little liquid, or oven too hot.
items.fix: Add a ladleful of warm stock or water when breaking the crust. The beans should always have some liquid covering them.

items.cause: Too much liquid at the surface, or breadcrumbs too coarse.
items.fix: Use fine dried breadcrumbs. If there is liquid pooling, break the crust and push it under; the next layer forms faster.

content.text: The rivalry between Carcassonne, Castelnaudary and Toulouse over the correct cassoulet recipe has been conducted with the seriousness of a diplomatic dispute for at least a century. Castelnaudary claims the original; Toulouse adds confit duck and mutton. This recipe sides with Castelnaudary's emphasis on the crust. Whatever the provenance, cassoulet is the great winter dish of the Languedoc, best made on a Saturday when there is nowhere to be until Sunday.
